data.title: Look, some economic data! UMich shows consumer mood unchanged near historical lows
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. stocks turn red, Nasdaq down most
  • Cons discretionary down most among S&P sectors; Cons staples leads gainers
  • Europe's STOXX 600 drops 1%
  • Dollar, bitcoin off; gold up; crude falls >3%
  • U.S. 10-year Treasury yield falls to 4.06%

LOOK, SOME ECONOMIC DATA! UMICH SHOWS CONSUMER MOOD UNCHANGED NEAR HISTORICAL LOWS

Ten days into the government shutdown, already investors are looking back on the halcyon days of official economic indicators with a sense of nostalgia. There was a time, they reminisce, when market participants - not to mention the Fed - had current data at their fingertips, which took much of the guesswork out of determining the state of the U.S. economy.

Fortunately, there are independent data sources like the University of Michigan (UMich), which informs us that the mood of the American consumer, who carries the burden of about 70% of the U.S. economy, has remained essentially unchanged this month.

The University of Michigan's (UMich) preliminary take on October Consumer Sentiment (USUMSP=ECI) shed a negligible 0.1 point to 55.0, more optimistic than the 54.2 consensus.

Survey participants' assessment of current conditions improved by 1.0%, while near-term expectations deteriorated by about 1.0%.

Still, lest folks prematurely pop the champagne cork, the headline number is the sixth most pessimistic reading in the survey's history.

"Overall, consumers perceive very few changes in the outlook for the economy from last month. Pocketbook issues like high prices and weakening job prospects remain at the forefront of consumers’ minds," says Joanne Hsu, Director of Consumer Surveys at UMich. "At this time, consumers do not expect meaningful improvement in these factors."

Near-term inflation expectations cooled by 10 basis points to a still-elevated 4.6%, or 1.5 percentage points hotter than the most recent core CPI print.

Longer term price growth expectations stood pat at 3.7%, still well above Powell & Co's average annual 2% inflation target.

"Inflation expectations for both time horizons are about midway between the readings seen a year ago and the highs seen this year in April and May in the wake of the initial announcements of major tariff changes," Hsu adds.

While consumer inflation expectations are not reliable predictors of the future path of price growth, they can and do affect consumer behavior. Fears of high inflation can prompt consumers to push purchases forward to avoid price hikes, inadvertently boosting demand and fulfilling their own prophecy.

(Stephen Culp)

*****

data.title: Credit Investors Eye Earnings Season to Weigh Tariff, Inflation Resilience — Market Talk
data.description: Credit investors' attention will be on third-quarter corporate earning releases due in the coming days, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. "The results should give us an idea of how companies are coping with tariffs, inflationary pressures, and the overall environment." Weaker-than-forecast earnings are likely to push credit spreads wider, he says. "We expect uninspiring results in Europe (much like the previous seasons) and better results in the U.S." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Germany Bund Yield Falls Amid US-China Trade Tensions
data.description:
The yield on Germany’s 10-year Bund tumbled to 2.63%, its lowest level since August 7, as escalating tensions between the US and China stoked fears of a global trade war.

US President Trump said on Friday there was no reason to meet with China’s Xi Jinping in two weeks in South Korea as planned, while preparing to raise tariffs on Chinese imports.

Investors also monitored political developments in France and the US, alongside disappointing German economic data.

President Emmanuel Macron is expected to appoint a new prime minister following Sébastien Lecornu’s resignation, with signs that snap elections are likely to be avoided.

The US government shutdown is expected to continue, threatening the release of key economic indicators and potentially affecting the Federal Reserve’s policy guidance.

In Germany, exports unexpectedly declined, while imports fell more sharply than anticipated, following earlier steep drops in industrial output and factory orders.

data.title: Energy Activity Falls Along With Profits, KC Fed Survey Finds — Market Talk
data.description: Energy activity in the middle of the U.S. remained negative in the third quarter and isn't expected to rebound in the next six months, the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City's quarterly survey shows. The Tenth District Energy Survey's key index of drilling and business activity comes in at minus 16 for the quarter, roughly in line with a second-quarter reading of minus 17, as revenue and profits fell to their lowest levels in two years. Readings above zero indicate expansion, while those below zero show contraction. The survey's future drilling and business activity index, which covers expectations for the next six months, comes in at 0 for a second straight quarter. (colin.kellaher@wsj.com)

data.title: US retailers slip as Trump hints at higher tariffs on China imports
data.description:
** Shares of U.S. retailers fall after President Donald Trump said the U.S. is calculating a massive increase in tariffs on Chinese imports, fueling concerns over likely impact on sourcing supplies

** Apparel retailers Gap , Nike , Ralph Lauren , Lululemon down ~2%

** Abercrombie & Fitch <ANF.N>, American Eagle Outfitters <AEO.N> down ~3%

** Shares of electronics retailer Best Buy <BBY.N> down ~4%

** Toymakers Mattel <MAT.O>and Hasbro <HAS.O> fall nearly 4%

** Levi Strauss down 12% after flagging high costs linked to U.S. import tariffs

** S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary Distribution & Retail Index .SPXRT is down 2.5% on Friday

data.title: Buy Sheds Direct Drops a Visual 'How to Build a Shed' Playbook with an Infographic as DIY Demand Keeps Climbing
data.description:
Birmingham, United Kingdom--(Newsfile Corp. - October 10, 2025) - Garden sheds are returning to the to-do list of every weekend, and search data tells its own tale. Buy Sheds Direct has published an updated version of their "How to Build a Shed: A Step-by-Step Guide" and a new one-page infographic to help anybody build a shed, even those who have no prior experience. From site preparation to finishing touches, this visual checklist covers everything.

Click here to see the full How to Build a Shed infographic.

To view an enhanced version of this infographic, please visit:

https://www.buyshedsdirect.co.uk/media/wysiwyg/pdf/how-to-build-a-shed-infographic.pdf

The guide has been updated to focus on the aspects people most struggle with. For instance, it discusses the shed base, frame, roof, and how best to avoid leaks. It includes shopping-list-ready materials, tools, and equipment; straightforward details on floor bearers, roofing felt, and EPDM; along with practical guidance on the common causes of door binding, typically due to uneven framing. It also provides clear advice on keeping windows watertight without excessive use of sealant.

The advantages and disadvantages of wooden, metal or plastic (resin) sheds regarding life span, ease of care, cost and level of security are explained. The complementary infographic summarises foundation options, types of roof covering, build sequence, mistakes to avoid and a nifty table comparing DIY-from-scratch, kit builds and pro installs.

"Many enjoy the thought of a weekend shed build until it comes to tackling the base," says a spokesperson at Buy Sheds Direct. "That stage has been made foolproof in the latest guide, which explains how to achieve a flat, square platform for shed installation using simple measuring and levelling tools, without the usual complications."

The guide continues to explain framing and roofs: a gentle push toward squaring as you go; framing windows with more studs so they don't shake; a reminder that roof coverings are not all created equal. Asphalt felt is cheap and is familiar to most people; bitumen shingles look sharp and last longer; EPDM rubber provides the most reliable waterproofing and years of not worrying about the roof. The infographic provides life spans and trade-offs side by side.

"Security is always the bit everyone plans to 'sort later', then winter comes, storms come and everywhere gets tested," Buy Sheds Direct added. "This update promotes early anchoring, appropriate drainage and fundamental lock placement because the simplest manoeuvres prevent 90% of mishaps." Households need to secure garden structures ahead of high winds; a shed that's screwed to a level base and fastened at the corners is not the one bouncing into next door's trampoline.

A recurring question is whether DIY is worth it. Price guides vary by size and base, but labour on a small 6x4 shed is often quoted in the low hundreds of £'s, rising with size and groundwork. Put bluntly, that's where DIYers save. The infographic pegs potential savings at around £700 in labour for straightforward jobs. The big money ends up in the base and assembly time. When the site is level and an extra pair of hands is available, the figures become far more favourable.

The updated guide also leans into permission and compliance, not as misery, just reality. Most garden sheds fall under permitted development in England, but there are height, placement and usage limits. For those intending to extend beyond standard limits, the Planning Portal provides clear guidance on the boundaries. Planning disputes with neighbours are seldom pleasant, particularly when a minor adjustment of 50cm could have prevented the issue entirely.

"People don't want jargon, they want to be able to understand everything clearly," Buy Sheds Direct comments. "That's why the guide picks up on the little things that are actually vital: raising timber up clear of damp ground, properly sealing each coat, putting a cheap gutter run beneath the bottom edge to avoid water sitting there."

The guide is provided in a traditional article format for those who prefer reading, covering materials, tools, ground preparation, fixation details, roof options, doors, windows, and a final list of finishes such as paint, sealant, shelving, hooks, and storage rails. The infographic provides a visual guide showing exactly what is needed to do on building day. From how to plan and prepare, to getting a foundation laid, frame walls and floor, fit doors and windows, roof installation and lastly, the finishing touches.

For those who have plans for a grand shed next spring, here are some of the top suggestions from Buy Sheds Direct:

  • Base first, base right - a flat, square, and level base is absolutely essential; it cannot simply be adjusted to fit individual needs. The guide compares concrete slabs, paving slabs, timber frames on blocks and plastic grid kits, including cost bands where each shine.

  • Measure twice, cut once, check plumb always - Re-checking with a spirit level means avoiding resetting, which prevents door rubbing and panel twist later.

  • Think through the roof properly - For gardens open to the elements, nasty winds and blistering summer sunshine, go for something more durable. It's cheaper than trying to fix everything later.

  • Basic safety is not negotiable - PPE and power-tool checks are dull until they aren't. The Health and Safety Executive exist for a reason.

The bigger picture: sheds have quietly become multi-purpose spaces, storage, hobby corners, tiny gyms and, therefore, not only is there more stuff inside, but also expect a higher level of finish outside. The updated guide meets with this reality in practical terms: lightweight plastic units are anchored down, timber should be treated early and regularly, if the metal sheets don't have any extra insulation, vents or windows can be added for airflow. It's practical, not precious.

Buy Sheds Direct has also included additional aftercare measures: guttering for runoff, a first-year inspection list (tighten fixings, re-seal cut ends, check for pooling), and simpler interior organisation to prevent long-handled tools at the back from falling when accessing stored items such as paint tins. Those habits keep a shed usable rather than becoming that guilty corner of the garden.

Both the guide and the infographic are free to take away. To buy a ready-made shed, Buy Sheds Direct's Shed Buying Guide is a handy place to compare sizes, materials, and roof types. This is useful even when building a shed from scratch, because it spells out trade-offs in plain English.

"Call it a British rite of passage," Buy Sheds Direct added. "Put up a decent shed once and it pays you back for many years to come. The aim is to make the first build process smoother, more cost-effective, and more efficient, with clear, practical guidance focused solely on getting the job done correctly.

About Buy Sheds Direct

Buy Sheds Direct is one of the UK's best-known garden building online retailers, supplying wooden, metal, and plastic sheds, storage, and accessories nationwide. The website provides straightforward product advice with practical build resources so customers can choose, build, and maintain with confidence. For media enquiries, email info@buyshedsdirect.co.uk.

Media Contact:

PR Manager, Buy Sheds Direct

Email: info@buyshedsdirect.co.uk

Phone: 0333 033 0514

Website: www.buyshedsdirect.co.uk

-ENDS-

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/269955

data.title: Euro Recovers Above $1.16 Amid Dollar Weakness
data.description:
The euro rebounded above $1.16 at the close of a volatile week, recovering from a more than two-month low of $1.154 touched on Thursday.

The recovery came as investors sold the dollar following US President Trump’s comments that there was no reason to meet with China’s Xi Jinping in two weeks in South Korea and his plans to raise tariffs on Chinese imports.

Markets also focused on political developments in France, where President Emmanuel Macron is expected to appoint a new prime minister after Sébastien Lecornu’s resignation.

Investors welcomed signs that snap elections are likely to be avoided, as Lecornu indicated that dissolving parliament was improbable.

On the monetary policy front, the ECB’s September meeting accounts showed that policymakers agreed current interest rates are sufficiently robust to address potential shocks amid two-sided inflation risks.

data.title: ICE Canola Midday: Heavy Pressure From Comparable Oils
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange were weaker by late Friday morning, as pressure from the Chicago soy complex intensified.

Losses in MATIF rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil added to canola's declines. A drop in crude oil pulled down the vegetable oils.

"There's a general negative sentiment in the markets today," an analyst said. "We're taking any weekly gains and negating them." Parts of the eastern Prairies are to get rain over the Thanksgiving weekend, while the western half of the region remains in need of sufficient moisture before freeze-up.

Saskatchewan said the canola harvest reached 89% finished province-wide, while the overall harvest hit 93% complete.

Canola exports continued to falter compared to those this time last year, the Canadian Grain Commission reported. Cumulative exports of the oilseed for the week ended Oct. 5 were 796,100 metric tons versus 1.95 million a year ago.

The Canadian dollar nudged up by mid-session Friday, with the loonie at 71.49 U.S. cents, compared with Thursday's close of 71.43.

The Canadian markets will be closed on Monday for Thanksgiving.

Approximately 41,250 canola contracts were traded as of 11:29 am EDT, with prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton:

 
           Price      Change 
Nov       605.60    dn 11.30 
Jan       620.50    dn 10.90 
Mar       631.90    dn 10.40 
May       642.20    dn  9.90

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1139 ET - CBOT grain futures are tumbling after President Trump posted on Truth Social that China is "becoming very hostile," and threatens to escalate the trade war and increase levies on Chinese goods. With a meeting between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping that was expected to happen in South Korea seemingly in jeopardy, the prospect of a trade deal allowing for U.S. agricultural exports to make it back to Chinese shores has darkened. This has racked up the pressure on CBOT grains, with most-active CBOT corn down 1.4%, soybeans down 1.9%, and wheat down 1.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1134 ET - Gold prices extend gains in evening trade, driven by a rush to safe-haven assets amid growing political and economic uncertainty. Gold futures in New York climb 1.5% to $4,0234.30 a troy ounce and are headed for a weekly gain of around 3% after President Trump threatened a "massive" tariff hike on imports of Chinese products, citing export controls on rare earths. Meanwhile, "while participants in other markets are waiting for new data from the U.S., gold is benefiting from the fact that none is being published, as this once again underscores the high level of political uncertainty," says Barbara Lambrecht from Commerzbank Research. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1129 ET - Financial markets make a sharp downturn as President Trump threatens China with "a massive increase" on tariffs. In a lengthy social-media post, Trump accuses Beijing of trying to "impose Export Controls on each and every element of production having to do with Rare Earths." The unexpected escalation of trade wars sends major stock index down around 1% or more, as investors seek refuge in Treasurys and yields deepen early declines, with the 10-year falling close to the 4% level. The WSJ Dollar Index is down 0.3% as the dollar weakens nearly 1% against the yen and 0.5% versus the euro. Gold is up 1% to $4,012 an ounce. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1125 ET - European stocks turn lower after President Trump said he is looking at "massive increase" in tariffs on Chinese goods being shipped to the U.S. and sees no reason to meet with his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping. The Stoxx Europe 600 is down 1%, taking a dive after Trump's comments signaled reviving trade tensions between the world's two largest economies. The Euro Stoxx 50--a narrower index of eurozone blue-chip stocks--is down 1.3%. Among national indexes, France's CAC 40 is down 1.3%, Germany's DAX down 1.1% and the U.K.'s FTSE 100 down 0.8%. "One of the Policies that we are calculating at this moment is a massive increase of Tariffs on Chinese products coming into the United States of America," Trump says in a Truth Social post. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1118 ET - Shares in European semiconductor companies are in decline after President Trump said he was considering a "massive increase" of tariffs and "many other countermeasures" on Chinese products coming into the U.S. Shares of Dutch chip-making equipment supplier ASML Holding and smaller rival ASM International are down more than 2%. Shares of BE Semiconductor Industries, the Dutch supplier of semiconductor assembly equipment, are down 3%. German chip maker Infineon Technologies logs a 2.6% decline. Shares of STMicroelectronics, which supplies to clients like Elon Musk's Tesla and Apple, fall 1.8%. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0921 ET - Travel by Canadians to the U.S. shows no sign of recovering as tensions between the country and its nearest neighbor remain high. Canadian-resident return trips from overseas increased 3.9% on-year in September, but air travel to the U.S. slumped 27.1%, Statistics Canada data shows. Return trips by automobile to the U.S. were down a ninth straight month, falling a steep 34.8%. The same month, non-resident trips to Canada by air rose a modest 1.9% on-year, though arrivals by U.S. residents slipped 0.4%. U.S.-resident trips to Canada by automobile declined 5.3%, an eight consecutive drop. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0852 ET - Retailers have slowed down on raising prices to offset tariffs, say analysts Brian Nagel and Andrew Chasanoff, citing an Oppenheimer analysis of 200 likely tariff-impacted items. As of Oct. 6, prices on 65 of the items were up compared with April 28 by an average of 17%. That is consistent with a previous reading in early September, suggesting price increases may be slowing down. Companies are still focused on protecting margins, but in the near-term, they may be wary of underlying consumer demand ahead of the holiday selling season and also may be waiting out potential court action on tariff policy, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0739 ET - U.S. tariffs are making some Europeans more concerned about their jobs, European Central Bank economists say in a blog post. About 15% of workers say they have a higher probability of job loss, with 3% saying that the chance of losing their jobs had increased a lot, according to a July ECB survey. The share of workers more afraid of job loss is higher in countries where the workforce is more exposed to U.S. exports, such as Ireland, the economists say. Workers in more trade-oriented and cyclical sectors, which are currently under pressure from other shocks like high energy prices, are also likely to believe their jobs are at risk. However, 85% of all workers shrug off concerns, reporting unchanged or even lower job-loss expectations, the economists add. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0633 ET - U.S. and European government bond yields fall, as the U.S. government shutdown continues, while in Europe investors wait for French President Emmanuel Macron's expected announcement of a new prime minister later in the day. "In the U.S., the government shutdown continued, delaying key economic data and complicating the Federal Reserve's policy outlook," XTB MENA's Milad Azar says in a note. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield falls 3.9 basis points to 4.108%; the 10-year German Bund yield is down 2.8bps to 2.677%; and the 10-year French OAT yield falls 3.7bps to 3.490%, according to Tradeweb data. U.K. gilt and Scandinavian government bond yields also trade lower. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0628 ET - China's expanded export controls on rare earths appear aimed at boosting its leverage in U.S. trade negotiations, Capital Economics says. President Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping are due to meet on the sidelines of an APEC summit in South Korea in a few weeks' time, economist Julian Evans-Pritchard says. "While the timing of the [rare earths] move may be opportunistic, we suspect the new controls are mostly motivated by medium-term geostrategic goals," CE says. China's stranglehold on rare earths won't last forever, as deposits outside the country are plentiful. But Beijing could shore up the position of its manufacturing sector and hold back foreign competitors before they manage to reduce reliance on Chinese supply, Evans-Pritchard says. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0410 ET - In government bond markets the vigilantes are restless, says AXA Investment Managers' Chris Iggo in a note. "There is a general discontent with how public finances are being managed," the CIO of AXA IM Core says. Populism and the associated political fragmentation tend to work against governments taking hard decisions on taxes and spending, says Iggo, who is also Chair of the AXA IM Investment Institute. "Note the current situation in France and the U.K.," he says. There is a bit of a stalemate in the bond markets with long yields stabilizing as investors wait for budget news in the U.K. and France and being placated in the U.S. by the prospect of lower Federal Reserve rates and ongoing growth, Iggo says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0400 ET - Gold prices slip back beneath the $4,000 mark, but remain on track for an eighth consecutive weekly gain as structural drivers stay in place. Futures in New York rise 0.2% to $3,982.10 a troy ounce in early trade and are up 1.9% on week. The retreat from record highs reflects profit-taking pressures and easing geopolitical risks after Israel signed off on President Trump's plan for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. However, any pullback is expected to be short-lived. The Federal Reserve is still expected to cut interest rates further, supporting non-yielding gold, while the prolonged U.S. government shutdown is injecting new uncertainty into markets. "If the shutdown does persist into next week, we won't get the CPI release on Wednesday, which will leave us increasingly uncertain about the U.S. economy," analysts at Deutsche Bank say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Grain Futures Tank After Trump Labels China 'Very Hostile' — Market Talk
data.description: CBOT grain futures are tumbling after President Trump posted on Truth Social that China is "becoming very hostile," and threatens to escalate the trade war and increase levies on Chinese goods. With a meeting between Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping that was expected to happen in South Korea seemingly in jeopardy, the prospect of a trade deal allowing for U.S. agricultural exports to make it back to Chinese shores has darkened. This has racked up the pressure on CBOT grains, with most-active CBOT corn down 1.4%, soybeans down 1.9%, and wheat down 1.8%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: IonQ slides after $2 bln stock-and-warrants sale
data.description:
** IonQ's shares down 6.9% at $72.19 on Fri after quantum computing firm announces $2 bln equity raise

** College Park, Maryland-based IONQ to sell ~21.5 mln shares, including ~5 mln pre-funded warrants, to Heights Capital Management

** Offering price of $93 is 20% above stock's last close on Thurs

** It will also sell 7-yr warrants to buy ~43 mln additional shares; warrants' exercise price of $155 is double the stock's last close

** Co intends to use net offering proceeds for working capital and other general purposes, and says may use a portion for strategic acquisitions and partnerships, according to the prospectus

** JP Morgan sole underwriter for the offering

** In July, IONQ sold $1 bln worth of stock and warrants to Susquehanna affiliate Heights Capital at offering price of $55.49

** Prior to latest offering, IONQ had ~330.9 mln shares outstanding for roughly $26 bln market cap through Thurs

** With move on Fri, shares up ~72% YTD and up ~175% over the past six months

** 6 of 8 analysts covering IONQ rate stock "buy", 2 have "hold", their median PT is $67.50, per LSEG data

data.title: Switzerland hopes to seal updated trade deal with China early next year
data.description:
Switzerland hopes to seal an updated free trade agreement with China in early 2026, Foreign Minister Ignazio Cassis said on Friday, following a meeting with his Chinese counterpart Wang Yi.

"We are pleased that the discussions on modernising (our free trade agreement) are proceeding constructively, and we both hope to be able to complete this work soon," Cassis told reporters after the pair met in Bellinzona.

"We will probably not achieve this by the end of this year as we had hoped, but we are counting on the beginning of next year for a more open, fair trade based on international free trade rules.” Cassis added.

data.title: Argentina bailout imperils the US purse either way
data.description:
By Sebastian Pellejero

Washington is propping up the peso with direct buys and a $20 bln swap line. The scale of a dollar crunch and the currency’s overvaluation make this a stopgap until a tough election for President Milei. Failure leaves Uncle Sam with losses. Success just invites further measures.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Sebastian Pellejero on LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

The U.S. government purchased Argentine pesos and finalized a $20 billion currency swap framework with Argentina's central bank, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on October 9.

data.title: Protagonist Therapeutics Leaps on Report of Possible J&J Deal
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Shares of Protagonist Therapeutics moved higher after The Wall Street Journal reported that Johnson & Johnson is in talks to buy company, citing people familiar with the matter.

Protagonist shares were recently up 9.4%, to $73.34.

A deal isn't guaranteed, and the details being discussed couldn't be learned, the people said, the Journal reported.

Protagonist sports a market capitalization topping $4 billion based on Thursday's closing price of $67.04 for the Newark, Calif., clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: PepsiCo Is Maintained at Equal-Weight by Wells Fargo
Chip Stocks Take Hit From Trump Threat to Ramp Up China Tariffs — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Semiconductor stocks tumbled Friday morning after President Trump threatened to increase tariffs on China.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell 3% after Trump posted the tariff threat in response to China's recent plans to restrict rare-earth materials. Among the chip related stocks taking hits today: Advanced Micro Devices, Nvidia, Broadcom, Micron Technology and Intel.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Bank of America, Goldman Sachs and other big banks ‘jointly exploring’ a stablecoin
data.description:
Some of the world's biggest and most powerful banks, including Bank of America, Goldman Sachs, and Citi, are considering joining forces to issue a stablecoin — even if they're not calling it that.

In a statement from Friday, BNP Paribas and other banks are "jointly exploring the issuance of a 1:1 reserve-backed form of digital money that provides a stable payment asset available on public blockchains, focused on G7 currencies."

Other banks mentioned in the group include Banco Santander, Barclays, Deutsche Bank, MUFG Bank Ltd, TD Bank Group, and UBS. The goal of their "initiative" is to determine if an "industry-wide offering could bring the benefits of digital assets and enhance competition across the market, while ensuring full compliance with regulatory requirements and best practice risk management," according to the statement.

Although large banks, including Bank of America, have expressed interest in issuing a stablecoin, especially after new legislation in the U.S. provided regulatory clarity, the market is currently dominated by crypto-native firms. Circle and Tether, which is planning a stablecoin specifically for the U.S. market, are the world's two largest issuers.

U.S. dollar-pegged stablecoins are the dominant token pegged to a fiat currency and backed by reserve assets. The USD stablecoin supply, at the moment, registers at $290 billion and continues to climb. Many have predicted that the stablecoin market will grow into the trillions of dollars in the coming years as institutions adopt the digital currency as a method of settling transactions.

The banks also said they are "in contact with regulators and supervisors in each relevant market and will continue to keep appropriate parties updated as the project progresses."
